title: Docker & Container Lifecycle
description: Docker is the most widely used container runtime, built on Linux cgroups and namespaces. At Enterprise, Docker (or its OCI-compatible alternatives like containerd) is the standard unit of deployment. Understanding the container lifecycle — from building to running to debugging — is essential for any infrastructure engineer.

sections.title: Container Images: Layers & Build Caching
sections.description: A Docker image is a stack of read-only layers, each representing a change to the filesystem. Layers are cached and shared between images — this is why pulling updates is fast (only new layers are downloaded). At Enterprise, image layer optimization is critical for deployment speed: minimizing layers, caching dependencies, and using distroless base images reduce both build time and attack surface.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Dockerfile best practices ===

# 1. Use specific base image versions (not 'latest')
FROM ubuntu:22.04@sha256:abc123...

# 2. Combine RUN commands to minimize layers
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \\
    python3 python3-pip \\
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# 3. Use .dockerignore (like .gitignore for builds)
# .dockerignore:
# node_modules
# .git
# *.log

# 4. Multi-stage builds (separate build deps from runtime)
FROM node:22 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --only=production

FROM node:22-alpine AS runtime
COPY --from=builder /app /app
USER node
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

# 5. Use distroless or alpine for smaller images
# Distroless: gcr.io/distroless/nodejs22-debian12
# Alpine: node:22-alpine (much smaller, but uses musl libc)

# 6. Don't run as root inside the container
RUN addgroup -S myapp && adduser -S myapp -G myapp
USER myapp

# === Build optimization ===
# Cache layers that change infrequently (package.json → deps first, then source)
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci            # This layer only rebuilds when package.json changes
COPY . .              # This layer rebuilds every time (frequent changes)

# === Layer inspection ===
docker history nginx              # Show layer sizes and commands
docker inspect nginx              # Detailed image metadata (layers, env, ports)

# === Image size commands ===
docker images --format "table {{.Repository}}\t{{.Tag}}\t{{.Size}}"
docker system df                  # Total disk usage of Docker

sections.title: Container Runtime & Lifecycle
sections.description: When Docker runs a container, it goes through: pull image → create container → create namespaces → set up cgroups → mount rootfs → apply seccomp → exec CMD. Understanding this sequence helps you debug startup failures and performance issues. At Enterprise, the container runtime is usually containerd (the industry-standard OCI runtime) with runc as the low-level runtime.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Container lifecycle commands ===
# Build:
docker build -t myapp:latest .       # Build image from Dockerfile
docker build --no-cache -t myapp .   # Force rebuild (ignore cache)

# Run:
docker run -d --name myapp -p 8080:80 myapp:latest
docker run -it --rm ubuntu:22.04 bash  # Interactive, auto-remove

# Stop/start:
docker stop myapp                    # SIGTERM + wait + SIGKILL
docker start myapp                   # Restart stopped container
docker restart myapp                 # Stop + start
docker kill myapp                    # SIGKILL immediately

# Inspect:
docker ps                            # Running containers
docker ps -a                         # All containers (including stopped)
docker logs -f myapp                 # Follow logs
docker logs --tail 100 myapp         # Last 100 lines
docker inspect myapp                 # Full container metadata (JSON)

# Execute in running container:
docker exec -it myapp bash           # Start shell in container
docker exec myapp ps aux             # Run command in container

# === Resource limits ===
docker run -d --name db \
  --memory=512M \
  --memory-reservation=256M \
  --cpus=0.5 \
  --pids-limit=200 \
  --ulimit nofile=65536:65536 \
  postgres:16

# === Health checks ===
# In Dockerfile:
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1

# Manual check:
docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' myapp

# === Copy files ===
docker cp myapp:/var/log/app.log ./local.log
docker cp ./config.yaml myapp:/etc/app/config.yaml

# === Cleanup ===
docker rm myapp                      # Remove container
docker rmi myapp:latest              # Remove image
docker system prune -a               # Remove ALL unused containers, images, networks

sections.title: Networking & Storage in Docker
sections.description: Docker provides several network drivers (bridge for single-host, overlay for multi-host, host for performance, macvlan for direct MAC assignment) and storage options (volumes for persistent data, bind mounts for host directories, tmpfs for temporary memory-backed storage). At Enterprise, overlay networks (Cilium, Flannel, Calico) handle container networking, and cloud block storage (EBS volumes) is mounted via bind mounts.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Network drivers ===
# Bridge (default): containers connect via docker0 bridge
docker network create mynet          # Create custom bridge network
docker run --network=mynet nginx

# Host: container uses host's network stack (no isolation, best performance)
docker run --network=host nginx

# Overlay: multi-host networking (Docker Swarm or Kubernetes)
docker network create -d overlay myoverlay

# === Port mapping ===
docker run -p 8080:80 nginx          # host:8080 → container:80

# === DNS within Docker ===
# Containers on same user-defined bridge can resolve each other by name
# docker run --name web --network=mynet nginx
# docker run --network=mynet alpine ping web  # Works!

# === Volumes ===
docker volume create mydata           # Create volume
docker run -v mydata:/var/lib/data postgres  # Mount volume

# Bind mount:
docker run -v /host/path:/container/path nginx

# tmpfs (RAM storage):
docker run --tmpfs /tmp:size=100M nginx

# === Backup volume ===
docker run --rm -v mydata:/source -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/mydata.tar.gz -C /source .

# === Storage drivers ===
# overlay2 (default, modern) — Copy-on-Write, fast
# aufs (legacy) — Older Ubuntu default
# devicemapper — RHEL legacy (slow, avoid)
docker info | grep Storage

# === Container resource inspection ===
docker stats                        # Live resource usage
# CPU%, MEM%, MEM USAGE/LIMIT, NET I/O, BLOCK I/O, PIDS

docker stats --no-stream --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.CPUPerc}}\t{{.MemUsage}}"

sections.title: Debugging Containers
sections.description: Containers introduce debugging challenges: you're running in an isolated environment with limited tools. At Enterprise, debugging containers requires understanding both the application inside and the host outside. Key tools: docker exec for inside, nsenter for entering namespaces from host, and cgroup inspections for resource issues.
sections.codeBlock:
# === Inside-container debugging ===
docker exec -it myapp bash            # Shell inside container

# Install debugging tools (temporary container):
docker run --rm -it --net=container:myapp --pid=container:myapp \
  nicolaka/netshoot:latest            # Network troubleshooting toolbox

# === Host-level debugging ===
# Find container PID:
CONTAINER_PID=$(docker inspect --format '{{.State.Pid}}' myapp)
echo $CONTAINER_PID

# Enter container namespaces from host:
sudo nsenter -t $CONTAINER_PID -n ip addr  # Network namespace
sudo nsenter -t $CONTAINER_PID -p ps aux   # PID namespace
sudo nsenter -t $CONTAINER_PID -a bash     # All namespaces

# Check container cgroup:
CGROUP_PATH=$(cat /proc/$CONTAINER_PID/cgroup | grep -oP '/system.slice/docker-.*\.scope')
cat /sys/fs/cgroup$CGROUP_PATH/memory.events

# === Container logs ===
docker logs myapp                     # All logs
docker logs --tail 50 --follow --timestamps myapp

# === Check container filesystem ===
docker diff myapp                     # Show changed files (A=added, C=changed, D=deleted)

# === Export container filesystem ===
docker export myapp > container.tar   # Export as tar archive
docker import container.tar           # Import as image

# === Debug entrypoint/cmd ===
# Override entrypoint to debug:
docker run -it --entrypoint sh myapp:latest

# === Common issues ===
# 1. Container exits immediately: check CMD/ENTRYPOINT and logs
# 2. Port already in use: change host port mapping
# 3. Out of disk space: docker system prune -a
# 4. Permission denied inside: check USER directive and volume permissions
# 5. DNS resolution fails: check --dns or /etc/docker/daemon.json

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Your containerized application is running slow in production. How do you debug?'

A: 'First, rule out resource limits. Check docker stats or the cgroup metrics: cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.stat (throttling), memory.events (OOM), io.stat (I/O throttling). If the container is hitting limits, increase them or optimize the app. Next, check the host: top for CPU, iostat for disk, free -h for memory. If the host is fine, debug inside the container: docker exec -it myapp bash, then use standard Linux tools (top, strace, lsof). If the container lacks tools, use nsenter from the host. At Enterprise, we use ephemeral debug containers — a sidecar with network and PID namespace sharing that contains all debugging tools, without modifying the production container.'

Q: 'How would you design a container image for production?'

A: 'Production container image requirements: (1) Use a specific base image version, pinned by digest, not tag. (2) Multi-stage build: builder stage with build tools, runtime stage with only the binary and minimal dependencies. (3) Non-root user — never run as root. (4) HEALTHCHECK instruction. (5) No secrets in the image — use Docker build secrets or runtime secrets injection. (6) Minimal size: use distroless or Alpine base, combined RUN layers, clean package manager cache. (7) Reproducible: exact same image for the same git commit. (8) Signed: use Docker Content Trust or cosign for image signing. At Enterprise, image compliance is enforced by admission controllers in the container registry — images without a HEALTHCHECK, running as root, or with known vulnerabilities are rejected.'

Q: 'What's the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT in a Dockerfile?'

A: 'ENTRYPOINT defines the executable that always runs. CMD provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT. ENTRYPOINT ["nginx"] + CMD ["-g", "daemon off;"] = runs nginx -g 'daemon off;'. If you override CMD at runtime (docker run nginx -t), it becomes nginx -t (CMD replaced). If you override ENTRYPOINT (docker run --entrypoint bash nginx), it runs bash instead of nginx. Best practice: use ENTRYPOINT for the fixed binary and CMD for the default arguments — this makes the container behavior predictable while allowing argument customization.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Dockerfile Best Practices — Official Docker documentation
  • Docker Compose — Multi-container orchestration (development & testing)
  • containerd documentation — Industry-standard container runtime used in Kubernetes

learningObjectives: Understand container Images: Layers & Build Caching

analogy.title: Docker as a Shipping Container
analogy.description: The image is a sealed container - same contents everywhere. Layers are stacked pallets - reuse common bases. The Dockerfile is the packing list. The daemon is the port authority. Namespaces isolate "this container sees only its own cargo."

commonMistakes.solution: Always use `rm -i` or `ls` first to confirm what will be deleted

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Container Images: Layers & Build Caching
practiceLabs.task: Run `python3` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man python3` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Container Runtime & Lifecycle

practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man docker` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: Networking & Storage in Docker
practiceLabs.task: Run `postgres` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man postgres` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `FROM` | Container Images: Layers & Build Caching |
| `RUN` | Container Images: Layers & Build Caching |
| `python3` | Container Images: Layers & Build Caching |
| `docker` | Container Runtime & Lifecycle |
| `postgres` | Container Runtime & Lifecycle |
| `HEALTHCHECK` | Container Runtime & Lifecycle |
| `docker` | Networking & Storage in Docker |
| `docker` | Debugging Containers |
| `nicolaka/netshoot` | Debugging Containers |
| `CONTAINER_PID` | Debugging Containers |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man python3`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: docker operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is Container Images: Layers & Build Caching?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The Container Images: Layers & Build Caching is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `python3` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the docker directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a docker directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a docker scenario, your monitoring alerts show python3 failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  Docker is the 'hello world' of containers — most FAANG environments use containerd or CRI-compatible runtimes directly, not the Docker CLI
FAANG, image building and deployment are entirely separate stages — building happens in a hermetic build environment, deployment pulls from a registry
productionTips: Production Tip:  Never run 'docker build' on a production server
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, docker & container lifecycle is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
